← the Atlas

R-4 — Local PTY daemon (@kolu/pty-host)

feature · budding ·accepted ·JUN 11, 2026

The redo plan for R-4 — local PTY daemonization. A1/A2 landed; the first Phase B (#1275) was built whole, verified live, and discarded for architecture. The redo: one rule (package boundary = process boundary = staleKey hash), a host-count-agnostic endpoint core, and two PRs — the door (daemon + topology flip, survival off), then survival — each fully FUNCTIONAL on deploy: every line must have a production caller (the no-dead-code rule that killed the three-PR draft). Carries the #1034 postmortem and its hard constraints.

Contents

  • The architecture — one rule, then a module map
    • Multi-host readiness (the shapes, not the feature)
  • What this makes impossible by construction
  • The two PRs — each an executable brief
    • B1 — the door: the daemon, spawned and consumed, survival off landed
    • B2 — survival the promise flips on
    • The gate — the second deploy (not a PR)
  • The #1034 postmortem — production failure and hard constraints
    • Hard constraints (binding on the redo)
    • Execution lessons (process, not product)
  • Design notes that carry forward
  • History

The R-4 plan of record — local PTY daemonization + @kolu/pty-host. Split out of the parent remote-terminals plan on 2026-05-30; the parent keeps R-1/R-1.5/R-1.6 (shipped) and R-2/R-3 (post-R-4), and now also owns the multi-host direction (1 local + N ssh-remote pty-hosts, host switching in the ChromeBar) that this plan must not foreclose. Current state: A1 ( #1055 ) and A2 ( #1063 ) are on master. The first Phase B build ( #1275 ) shipped the full feature as one 40-commit PR, was verified live in production, and was then deliberately discarded: functional, but the architecture was discovered in review rather than designed. This note is the redo plan — same user-facing functionality, a designed architecture, and a two-PR split sized so each PR can be executed end-to-end by a single agent session. (A first draft split this into three PRs, leading with “the daemon binary, inert” — built as #1287 and then halted: an inert binary with no production caller is dead code wearing a green CI badge. The split below is the repair; #1287 is held and will be repurposed into B1.)

Companions: the srv · pty ChromeBar rail (A2’s deliverable, shipped) and kolu-tui (Phases 0–2 shipped — the CLI that dials the pty-host unix socket, today served by the in-process server, after B1 by the daemon).

The architecture — one rule, then a module map

The one rule everything follows from: the package boundary IS the process boundary IS the staleKey hash set. @kolu/pty-host contains exactly the code that executes inside the daemon — the PTY primitive, the wire contract, the taps, the socket serving, and the process entry (pid-gate acquisition, the daemon’s own root/shellDir, env application, exit handling). The staleKey is “the nix hash of this package dir” with zero file-level exceptions, and buildId.closure.test.ts walks the import graph from the daemon entry (not index.ts) — the closure test now answers the exact question the staleKey asks: what would a restart gain? Everything that supervises from outside (spawn, wait, handle, restart, reconcile) lives in packages/server — un-hashed, correctly, because changing the supervisor never changes what a restart would gain.

The remaining axes, most stable → most volatile (the boundaries that own them):

AxisRateBoundary that owns it
Everything in the daemon processrarely@kolu/pty-host — hashed whole; the entry travels with the package, so R-2’s remote daemon gets single-instance arbitration for free.
Wire contract + compat + identityper wire changeptyHostSurface + PTY_HOST_CONTRACT_VERSION + PtyHostIdentity, in-package (unchanged from A1/A2).
Identity values: staleKey vs navigableCommitkey: per wire change · commit: per deploybuildId.ts (unchanged). Currency is derived at the read site (staleKey !== currentBuildId()), never stored, never frozen onto a connection.
Endpoint status (the one health owner)per endpoint-model changeserver/src/ptyHost/endpoint.ts — emits {state, identity, startedAt} on every transition; a map keyed by hostId (a map of one, today: local). Everything else — buildInfo, rail, degraded canvas — derives by subscription.
Local reach mechanicsper platform quirklocalDriver.ts — systemd-run --user with per-spawn unique unit names + explicit --setenv env forwarding (Linux) / detached+unref (macOS); waitForPidGone (ESRCH poll, load-aware ceiling); pid-gate read side. R-2 adds sshDriver as a sibling (HostSession reach + provisionAgent).
Recovery sequenceper recovery policyrestart.ts — one composed capture → save → drain → waitForGone → respawn → reattach, all steps non-optional in the type, serialized (concurrent callers observe restarting), parameterized over driver ops. The forced and user paths are the same function. Ships in B2, where every step has a real caller; B1’s boot recycle is a plain inline sequence until then.
Reconciliation + adoptionper persisted fieldreconcile.ts (a pure function: daemon.list() × savedSession → {adopt, restoreCard, orphanExtras}) + wireTerminal in local.ts with adopt and spawn as siblings. Adoption consumes the whole SavedTerminal record as a unit — never field-by-field reconstruction — and a schema-level round-trip test iterates the schema’s keys.
Health → UI projectionper UX tweakA per-host status collection on the surface; rail + DegradedCanvas + restart dialog subscribe. User-facing name: “kolu daemon”.

There is deliberately no @kolu/pty-host-daemon package. The supervisor has no second consumer — R-2’s ssh driver is a sibling behind the endpoint concept, not a consumer of the local driver’s code. A directory seam gives the same review isolation; promote it to a package only when R-2 produces a real second tenant.

Multi-host readiness (the shapes, not the feature)

The parent note owns the direction: 1 local + N ssh-remote pty-hosts, host switching in the ChromeBar, reattach to all of them. Phase B ships exactly one endpoint but must be host-count-agnostic in its shapes: the endpoint map is keyed by hostId; daemon status is a per-host collection (not a singleton cell); adoption and the saved session join on (host, id) — terminal records already carry location from R-1; and the daemon-side code is one hashed package that runs identically on a remote host. None of this costs more than the singleton version; retrofitting any of it later costs another #1275.

What this makes impossible by construction

The production failures — four from #1034, four more paid for during #1275 — each become a failing test at the phase that owns the concept:

Production failureKilled atHow
Mis-scoped staleness key (wire change didn’t flip it)A2 ✅Closure-scoped key + import-walk guard (re-rooted at the daemon entry in B1).
Over-prompting (key nudged on every deploy)A2 ✅Server-only change leaves the key bit-identical — a falsifiable test.
Data-loss restart (kill-then-pray, #1034)B2One composed restart, snapshot-before-kill a type-level fact; forced + user paths are one function.
Empty-canvas lie (dead daemon → “no terminals”)B1Honest dead/degraded state ships with the door, before any survival promise exists.
Lossy adoption (#1275: splits un-nested, then agent-resume lost and autosaved, poisoning cold restores)B2Adoption consumes the whole persisted record; a schema-key-iterating round-trip test closes the class, not the instance.
Lazy adopt-on-spawn (#1275: orphan shells respawned into the daemon — duplicated terminals)B2Eager boot-time reconciliation is the only adopt path; orphans are reaped and logged, non-survivors go to the restore card — never silently dropped.
Identity gone stale after restart (#1275: one-shot read, then manual republish at enumerated call sites)B1One status owner emitting on every transition; everything derives by subscription.
Contract-skew crash-loop masked by launchd as an “App updated” loop (the zest field report on #1275 )B1Version checked over the socket on every connect, never an import-time throw; skew at boot → controlled recycle (B1) / composed restart (B2).
Dead code shipped ahead of its consumer (the halted first B1, #1287 )the split itselfThe no-dead-code rule: every line a PR adds must name the production code path that executes it on the first deploy — tests don’t count.

The two PRs — each an executable brief

No feature flags — each PR ships complete to master and leaves kolu working. Two rules govern the split, and the second exists because the first draft violated it:

  1. The hazard rule: each PR must be complete w.r.t. the hazards it opens — and the door can open with the survival promise off, which empties its hazard set by policy.
  2. The no-dead-code rule: for every line a PR adds, you must be able to name the production code path that executes it on the first deploy. Tests don’t count; “exercised by vitest” is not functional. This rule killed the draft’s standalone “daemon binary, inert” PR (built as #1287 , halted): there is no honest way to make the binary functional without its consumer — kolu-tui doesn’t need it (the in-process server serves that socket), and a daemon beside the in-process host is a split-brain serving zero terminals. The binary and the topology flip are one functional unit. The same rule binds within each PR: no speculative exports (an index.ts re-export with no importer is dead), no typed sequences shipped with degenerate no-op steps, no staged-for-later helpers — each arrives in the PR where its first real caller lives.

Each brief below is written to be executed end-to-end by a single agent session; the Traps lists are load-bearing — they encode the exact failure modes already paid for.

B1 — the door: the daemon, spawned and consumed, survival off landed #1287

Rough size: ~1,500 LoC (≈700 already on the held branch: pid-gate + entry + their tests; ≈800 new: driver + endpoint + composition root + flip + rail column + e2e isolation — roughly half of it tests). For calibration, #1275’s whole-feature diff was ~2,965 LoC, ~58% mechanism.

Goal: the daemon exists and the server runs on it — one PR, everything live on deploy. The server spawns the daemon at boot and becomes its client over the unix socket, while user-facing semantics stay byte-identical to today: the boot policy is always recycle (connect-if-survivor → kill → waitForPidGone → spawn fresh → connect). No survivors exist, so no survival hazard can open: no orphans, no skew older than one boot, nothing for a restart to pray over. Every production deploy now exercises kill → wait-for-real-exit → respawn — the exact race #1034 lost — with zero sessions at stake. User-visible increment: the rail’s DAEMON column lights up with the daemon’s own uptime and state. (Base: the held #1287 branch — its pid-gate, daemonMain, closure-test re-root, and nix wrapper are this PR’s first half, correct and tested; what follows adds their production caller.)

Deliverables — the daemon side (@kolu/pty-host; every file hashed into the staleKey):

  • packages/pty-host/src/daemonMain.ts — the process entry, a plain sequence: acquire the pid-gate atomically (write pid to a temp file, link(2) it into place; on EEXIST read the gate, liveness-probe kill(pid,0), exit 0 if a live daemon holds it, unlink-and-retry if stale) → compute the daemon’s own root + shellDir with an in-package helper (do not import the server’s koluRoot) → serve via servePtyHost + servePtyHostOverUnixSocket at getPtyHostSocketPath() → emit one structured boot log line → on SIGTERM: close the socket, release the gate, exit. PTY env derives from the daemon’s own process.env (the cleanEnv/koluIdentityEnv/prepareShellInit calls already live daemon-side in servePtyHost’s spawn handler).
  • The pid-gate module: acquire (daemon-side) and read (server-side discovery) — one pidGatePathForSocket derivation, never two.
  • buildId.closure.test.ts re-rooted at the daemon entry (walking index.ts and daemon.ts); every file in the package hashed and reachable-or-test — zero exceptions.
  • The kolu-pty-host bin (nix wrapper carrying the identity env; pty-host is already in the build fileset).
  • Tests: gate-race choreography (A acquires → B exits 0 while A lives → A dies → C acquires); double-bind hits the socket’s already-served refusal as a second tripwire; system.version roundtrip over the socket.

Deliverables — the server side (packages/server/src/ptyHost/; what makes every line above live):

  • localDriver.ts — spawn via systemd-run --user gated on INVOCATION_ID, per-spawn unique unit name, absolute daemon path, explicit --setenv forwarding of the daemon-env keys, dev-flag exec-arg filter; macOS detached+unref. waitForPidGone.ts — poll kill(pid,0) → ESRCH; load-aware ceiling, ~120s default.
  • endpoint.ts — the one health owner: state machine connecting | connected | degraded | dead, emits {state, identity, startedAt} on every transition; a map keyed by hostId (a map of one, today). The boot recycle lives here as a plain inline sequence — kill → waitForPidGone → spawn → connect. Not the composed typed restart: that abstraction ships in B2 where its capture/drain/reattach steps have real callers (rule 2).
  • Composition root: index.ts boots explicitly — parse argv → ensureLocalEndpoint() → construct LocalTerminalBackend with the injected client → listen. server/src/ptyHost.ts’s import-time construction is deleted; no module-global client, no top-level await.
  • The flip: LocalTerminalBackend’s client is the endpoint’s socket-backed client; the server stops serving the pty-host socket (today’s index.ts:397-407) — the daemon serves it; kolu-tui is unchanged.
  • Contract handshake on every connect (isContractVersionCompatible against system.version); skew at boot → recycle (the boot policy anyway). Never an import-time version throw.
  • Honest minimal state: a per-host daemon status on the surface; the rail’s DAEMON column lights up (uptime from the daemon’s startedAt beside SRV’s, green/red dot); a mid-session daemon death presents as an explicit degraded state — visibly distinct from “you have no terminals”.
  • e2e: per-worker XDG_RUNTIME_DIR socket isolation; hooks teardown kills spawned daemons.

Traps: no mode flag — the socket is the path (a KOLU_DAEMON_MODE toggle is a feature flag in a trench coat); do not implement adoption (assert terminal.list() is empty after the recycle); do not widen any shared schema; restore-card-on-deploy behavior must be exactly today’s; no import-time throws, no top-level await, nothing in @kolu/pty-host may import from packages/server; every export added must have a named production importer in this PR (rule 2 — e.g. no index.ts re-export of the serving entry until R-2 actually imports it; daemonMain uses the relative path).

Verify: e2e green on both platforms (the darwin lane’s presence is itself a check — its absence was once silent); kolu-tui works against the daemon; pkill-ing the daemon mid-session shows the honest dead state; a staged deploy shows the recycle in the journal; a server-only change leaves KOLU_PTY_HOST_BUILD_ID bit-identical across two nix builds, touching daemonMain.ts flips it.

B2 — survival the promise flips on

Rough size: ~1,100 LoC (≈250 reconcile/adopt domain delta + ≈200 composed restart + ≈350 UX: nudge, confirm dialog, palette command, DegradedCanvas + ≈300 tests incl. the two e2e features).

Goal: replace the boot recycle with reconciliation; ship the supervised restart and the currency UX. This is the domain-dense PR — smaller in mechanism, the densest in review — and everything it composes is already merged and prod-soaked. The composed restart abstraction is introduced here, where all of its steps have real callers.

Deliverables:

  • reconcile.ts — pure function (daemonList, savedSession) → {adopt, restoreCard, orphanExtras}. Boot uses it when the handshake passes; daemon-side PTYs not in the saved session are killed and logged; saved terminals not surviving go to the restore card — never silently dropped. One structured diagnostic log from day one: reattach: {daemonPtys, adopting, fromSaved, orphanedSaved, extras}.
  • Adoption: wireTerminal as the shared core, adopt and spawn as siblings over it. Adopt consumes the whole SavedTerminal (parentId so splits stay nested, lastAgentCommand so a later cold restore still offers claude -c, and every other server-persisted field), marks ready from the list entry’s pid (no spawn RPC), re-runs the provider DAG fresh against the surviving taps. The round-trip test iterates the persisted schema’s keys — every field survives adopt→snapshot.
  • restart.ts — the composed sequence, introduced here with every step real: capture (snapshotSession → setSavedSession, which wins the autosave-cancel race by construction) → drain (killAndUnwatch per terminal — abort the exit tap then kill, the single kill path) → waitForPidGone → respawn → reconnect → reattach. All steps non-optional in the type; serialized (concurrent triggers observe restarting); a failed respawn leaves dead + the preserved session + a retry affordance — never an empty canvas. Exposed as the daemon.restart RPC; B1’s inline boot recycle is absorbed into it (the boot path is now a caller too, so no degenerate steps exist).
  • Forced path: contract-skew on adopt → the same composed restart with an empty capture.
  • Currency UX: staleKey !== currentBuildId() derived at read sites; the rail’s ⬆ update pending (amber) is a one-click confirmed restart whose dialog explains why (the daemon is a build behind), what happens (a brief terminal recycle with the session restored), and that it’s optional; a palette command Restart kolu daemon; full DegradedCanvas treatment; a tip in the tips registry; changelog.
  • e2e: daemon-reattach.feature + daemon-update.feature, written red-first where the harness allows.

Traps: never key currency on the commit hash (the #1034 over-prompting bug, re-introduced once already); never store currency as a boolean; never rebuild adopted records field-by-field through the spawn path’s metadata seeding — the record flows whole; never let the nudge fire on a server-only deploy (that’s the falsifiable A2 test, now end-to-end).

Verify: the unit ordering invariant (capture < save < drain < waitForGone < respawn < reattach, and restart.ts does not compile with a step omitted); the schema round-trip test; both e2e features; then the gate below.

The gate — the second deploy (not a PR)

CI structurally cannot exercise “redeploy over a live daemon”; the acceptance signal is a staged prod checklist, a planned step: deploy B2 → open terminals + a running agent + a split → server-only redeploy (no nudge; survivors adopted; the reattach: counts reconcile; the split still nested; the agent still running) → pty-host-touching redeploy (amber nudge → confirmed restart → recovery honest; a deliberately failed respawn leaves the restorable session).

Two honest costs, accepted up front

1 — B1’s recycle can block boot. On a loaded box, the old daemon’s exit can take minutes (#1034’s 25G/2min case); B1’s boot waits on waitForPidGone up to the load-aware ceiling before spawning. That is the same blast radius as today’s in-process teardown — and it is exactly the soak we want on the race before B2 promises anything. 2 — rollback orphans a daemon. Rolling back B1 or B2 leaves a surviving daemon holding the socket; the in-process server’s own socket serve will refuse politely (already-served) and kolu-tui will keep talking to the orphan. The reap is manual and documented: systemctl —user stop the unit (Linux) / kill the pid in the gate (macOS) — the mirror of the A1 cutover reap.

Open follow-up — surface ↔ pty-host upstreaming

The first instalment shipped with kolu-tui Phase 1 ( #1084 ): the unix-socket transport pair, getRuntimeSocketPath, serveOverStdio’s settled end result, and the generic isContractVersionCompatible all moved into @kolu/surface. What remains for a post-B pass: a standard system.version/build-id handshake fragment, plus whatever B1’s endpoint glue turns out to want.

The #1034 postmortem — production failure and hard constraints

The first R4c-UI build ( #1034 ) shipped the build-mismatch “update pending” nudge + a Restart local PTY daemon command. On production (Linux/systemd, 2026-05-30) the nudge fired correctly on a deploy; clicking restart then destroyed a live 20-terminal session and could not bring the daemon back. From the journal: killAll drained all 20 terminals first; the old daemon (13.5h old, 25G RAM on a thrashing box) took ~2min to exit; the respawn timed out at 30s; the user was left with zero terminals, a dead daemon handle, and an empty canvas indistinguishable from “you have no terminals.” No second daemon ever ran — the failure was the respawn losing the race against the slow old-daemon exit.

Hard constraints (binding on the redo)

  1. Restart must be recoverable, never kill-then-pray. Snapshot the session first; after respawn, auto-reattach/offer restore; if respawn fails, a loud recoverable degraded state with the session preserved — never an empty canvas.
  2. Wait for the old daemon to fully exit — the single-instance lock fights the restart. Wait on actual process exit (kill(pid,0) → ESRCH) before spawning, with generous load-aware ceilings.
  3. Timeouts must fit a loaded production box, not an idle dev one (20 heavy PTYs + a tsx cold-start under swap ≫ 30s).
  4. Never lie about state — an explicit connecting/degraded UI, never silent emptiness.
  5. A persistent PTY status indicator, always visible — connected · connecting · degraded/dead · update-pending (became A2’s rail).
  6. Key staleness on pty-host, not the whole binary — hash the @kolu/pty-host source closure so the nudge fires only when a restart actually gains something (became A2’s staleKey; B1 re-roots the closure at the daemon entry).
  7. Back up the session before a destructive restart (Export/Import session shipped via #1046 ).
  8. Fix the preferences storm ( #1041 , fixed in #1050 — coalesced writes).

Execution lessons (process, not product)

From building + salvaging #1034, re-confirmed during #1275: typecheck every package before commit (a clean nix build is not proof CI passes, #1049 ); a new workspace package has a checklist (default.nix explicit fileset, staleKey-closure decision, lockfile, vitest wiring) — the fileset miss broke CI once already; never git stash in the worktree; grep for unresolved conflict markers before committing a merge; never defer with a someday-issue — gaps go into the plan and get done in the PR; when the terminal contradicts itself, confirm via an independent path before “fixing” a phantom. Wire-shape rules: additive wire fields are optional + no contract bump (a required field force-restarts a surviving older daemon just to add a diagnostic); the identity surfaced to users is the navigable one (commit SHA), with the source hash kept as the staleness key only. And the #1275-specific one: CI-green is necessary, not sufficient — the second deploy is the acceptance signal; budget the staged-prod loop as a planned step with the diagnostic logging built in advance.

Design notes that carry forward

The survivor is @kolu/pty-host only — node-pty fds + the @xterm/headless mirror + the raw VT taps + a unix socket + its own process entry. A kolu-server restart re-runs the providers against the surviving PTYs, so detection is never stale while the PTYs persist. Honest cost (accepted): metadata is no longer “warm” across a restart — a brief re-detection pass, trading warm-on-reconnect for freshness-on-deploy. Only a pty-host contract change (rare) forces terminal loss; a provider change (frequent) restarts the cheap layer with PTYs untouched. This corrects #1031, which daemonized a survivor that held the providers.

The cgroup mechanism (spike-verified). On Linux/systemd the daemon spawns via systemd-run --user (gated on INVOCATION_ID), landing in its own transient cgroup — a plain detached/setsid child does not survive on cgroup-v2 (KillMode=control-group walks cgroup membership, not the session — the #1031 Linux failure). macOS’s detached spawn already survives launchd. Caveats folded into B2: linger must be on; absolute daemon path (minimal unit PATH); per-spawn unique unit names (a dead unit can linger loaded). Single-instance two ways: the unit name plus the atomic pid-gate.

tmux/dtach considered and rejected: they only keep a PTY alive — no OSC-parsed taps, no headless snapshot for lazy-attach, no home for the provider DAG. You’d still build pty-host’s streaming layer next to tmux and inherit its session model on top.

History

  • R4a #1023 + R4b #1028 shipped clean as pure in-process refactors.
  • Two prototypes seeded the design: #994 (remote) proved “providers in the agent”, re-provisioned fresh per version; #1010 was a PTY-only local daemon — the thin-survivor model. The redo is their correct combination.
  • R4c first attempt #1031 dropped after a macOS prod failure: it daemonized pty-host plus the provider DAG, so the survivor served stale detection on every deploy — and staleness keyed on the inert pkgVersion constant, so outdated was always false and a 20-hour-old daemon was reused forever.
  • R4c redo #1034 — the postmortem above; shipped + salvaged, then discarded by the fresh approach.
  • The fresh-approach redo (2026-05-30/31): #1055 (A1) landed the in-process foundation and removed every #1034 daemon artifact; #1059 unified the link family; #1063 (A2) landed identity + the closure-hash guard + the rail. kolu-tui Phases 0–2 ( #1073 / #1084 / #1255 ) then battle-tested the socket transport in production.
  • Phase B first build #1275 (2026-06-11) — the whole feature as one 40-commit PR: functional, verified live across staged prod deploys, and discarded. What it proved: the user-facing spec works end-to-end. What it taught (each now a row in the impossible-by-construction table): the @kolu/pty-host-daemon package was extracted mid-PR after the review gauntlet; reattach correctness arrived as three sequential prod fixes (parentId, contract-on-adopt, lastAgentCommand); identity freshness was patched three times; the staleKey hash needed three lockstep file-level exception sites. The redo above designs each of those in.
  • The redo plan, first draft (2026-06-11): a three-PR split — “daemon binary, inert” → “the door, survival off” → survival. Its first PR was built ( #1287 : the pid-gate, daemonMain, the closure-test re-root, the nix wrapper — correct and tested) and then halted at review: the binary had no production caller, violating the requirement that every phase ships functional increments. The draft’s loophole was the phrase “inert-but-exercised mechanism” — tests exercising code is not functionality.
  • This plan (2026-06-11, revised): the two-PR redo — B1 (the door: daemon + supervisor + topology flip, survival off) → B2 (survival) → the staged-prod gate — governed by the no-dead-code rule above.
  • B1 landed #1287 (the repurposed held branch): the daemon half (entry · pid-gate · closure-test re-root · nix wrapper) plus the server half that makes it live — ptyHost/localDriver.ts (systemd-run --user own-cgroup spawn with --setenv forwarding + the dev-flag filter / macOS detached), ptyHost/waitForPidGone.ts, ptyHost/endpoint.ts (the one status owner: boot recycle → connect → handshake → heartbeat), the explicit composition root in index.ts (the server spawns + injects the socket client, stops serving the socket itself), the daemonStatus cell + the rail’s live pty column with uptime, and per-worker e2e daemon isolation. Verified by a real-daemon endpoint integration test (spawn → connect → recycle → survive) and a hand smoke (kolu-tui keeps working after the server is killed). Next: B2.
← the Atlas github ↗
